title: Numbers, Days & Time
situation: Foundation: counting, days of the week, months, and telling the time

phrases.hindi: een, twee, drie, vier, vijf, zes, zeven, acht, negen, tien
phrases.english: one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten
phrases.context: The core numbers ‚Äî drill these until they're automatic. 'Twee' rhymes with 'way', 'vijf' sounds like 'vayf'
phrases.pronunciation: AYN, tway, DREE, feer, VAYF, zes, ZAY-ven, acht, NAY-gen, teen

phrases.hindi: twintig, dertig, veertig, vijftig, zestig, zeventig, tachtig, negentig, honderd, duizend
phrases.english: twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, sixty, seventy, eighty, ninety, hundred, thousand
phrases.context: 'Tachtig' (80) is irregular ‚Äî from 'acht'. 'Duizend' = thousand. Compound numbers: 'eenentwintig' = 21 (one-and-twenty)
phrases.pronunciation: TWIN-tich, DER-tich, VEER-tich, VAYF-tich, ZES-tich, ZAY-ven-tich, TACH-tich, NAY-gen-tich, HON-derd, DOW-zend

phrases.hindi: maandag, dinsdag, woensdag, donderdag, vrijdag, zaterdag, zondag
phrases.english: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday
phrases.context: Dutch week starts Monday. 'Woensdag' (Wednesday) is tricky ‚Äî the 'oe' sounds like English 'oo', so it's WOONS-dach. Abbreviated: ma, di, wo, do, vr, za, zo
phrases.pronunciation: MAAN-dach, DINS-dach, WOONS-dach, DON-der-dach, VRAY-dach, ZAA-ter-dach, ZON-dach

phrases.hindi: januari, februari, maart, april, mei, juni, juli, augustus, september, oktober, november, december
phrases.context: Very similar to English. 'Maart' = March, 'Mei' = May, 'Juli' = July. Months are lowercase in Dutch.
phrases.pronunciation: ya-new-AA-ri, feb-ru-AA-ri, maart, ap-RIL, may, YU-ni, YU-li, ow-GUS-tus, sep-TEM-ber, ok-TOH-ber, no-VEM-ber, de-SEM-ber

phrases.hindi: Hoe laat is het? Het is twee uur. Kwart over twee. Half drie. Kwart voor drie.
phrases.english: What time is it? It is two o'clock. Quarter past two. 2:30. Quarter to three.
phrases.context: CRITICAL quirk: 'half drie' = HALF past two (2:30), NOT half past three! Dutch count toward the next hour. 'Kwart over twee' = 2:15. 'Kwart voor drie' = 2:45
phrases.pronunciation: hoo laat is het? het is tway uur. kwart OH-ver tway. half dree. kwart vohr dree.

phrases.hindi: Op welke dag? Vandaag is het maandag. Gisteren was het zondag. Morgen is het dinsdag.
phrases.english: On which day? Today is Monday. Yesterday was Sunday. Tomorrow is Tuesday.
phrases.context: 'Vandaag' (today), 'gisteren' (yesterday), 'morgen' (tomorrow). These are your most-used time words.
phrases.pronunciation: op WEL-ke dach? van-DAACH is het MAAN-dach. CHIS-ter-en was het ZON-dach. MOR-gen is het DINS-dach.

phrases.hindi: In welk jaar? In tweeduizend drie√´ntwintig. Welke datum? De vijfde mei.
phrases.english: In which year? In 2023. What date? The fifth of May.
phrases.context: Dates: 'de vijfde mei' (the fifth of May). For years: 'tweeduizend drie√´ntwintig' ‚Äî say each part separately. Current year: 'tweeduizend zesentwintig'
phrases.pronunciation: in welk yaar? in tway-DOW-zend DREE-en-twin-tich. WEL-ke DAA-tum? de VAYF-de may.

grammar_notes: Dutch compound numbers are written as one word and said smallest-first: 21 = eenentwintig (one-and-twenty), 45 = vijfenveertig (five-and-forty)
CRITICAL: 'half + next hour' = 30 minutes before that hour. 'Half drie' = 2:30. Always think of the next hour when you hear 'half'
'Kwart' means quarter. 'Kwart over' = quarter past, 'kwart voor' = quarter to
Days and months are always lowercase in Dutch, unlike English
culture_notes: Dutch people are precise about time ‚Äî being on time is important. 'Stipt op tijd' = exactly on time
The 24-hour clock is commonly used in official contexts (trains, appointments): 'Het is veertien uur dertig' = 14:30
When making appointments, Dutch people often check 'de agenda' (diary/calendar) before committing

skill_breakdown.explanation: Dutch numbers follow a clear pattern. 1-12 are irregular and must be memorized. 13-19 add '-tien' (teen). 20-90 have their own words. Compound numbers (21-99) flip the order: unit + 'en' + ten.

Dutch uses a half-to system. Think of the clock face: 'half drie' means you are halfway to 3:00, so it's 2:30. For :15 and :45, use 'kwart over' and 'kwart voor'. For times like 2:20, say 'tien over half drie' (ten past half three = 2:20).

practice_prompt: You are a Dutch tutor drilling numbers, dates, prices, and time-telling with a beginner. Use 'u' throughout (polite tutor register). Run a tight 6-8 item drill, one task at a time: say a number aloud, read a price in euros, give a time, read a date, and back-convert. Open with: 'Goedendag! Laten we beginnen met cijfers. Eerste oefening: zeg in het Nederlands het getal 47.' Wait for the student's response, score it briefly, then escalate to the next item. Focus the grammar specifically on (a) the 21+ backwards inversion ‚Äî `zevenenveertig`, NOT `veertigenzeven`, with the `en` connector intact ‚Äî (b) half-hour times running backwards (`half drie` = 2:30, not 3:30), (c) day-month-year date order, and (d) lowercase days and months. When the student errs, flag it inline with `[[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]]`. Watch for: non-inversion (English-order `veertigzeven`), wrong half-hour (`half drie` meaning 3:30), capitalized day/month names, and dropping the `en` connector (`zeven veertig`). Stay in Dutch unless the student explicitly asks for English. After 6-8 items, close with: 'Heel goed! Tot ziens.'

theory.intro: Dutch numbers look tame until you hit 21 ‚Äî at which point the structure flips and stays flipped through 99. Combined with `half past` running backwards (`half drie` = 2:30, halfway TO three), this is the foundation where English speakers' instincts hurt most. Once you've drilled the inversion and the time-telling logic, everything else (dates, days, prices) is mechanical.

sections.heading: The basics: 1-20
sections.body:
The first ten Dutch numbers map cleanly onto English ‚Äî `een, twee, drie, vier, vijf, zes, zeven, acht, negen, tien`. Drill these until they're as automatic as your own phone number. Pronunciation is the only trap: `vijf` rhymes with `safe`, `acht` ends with the throaty Dutch `ch`.

From 11 to 19 the pattern is teen-like but irregular at the start. `Elf` (11) and `twaalf` (12) are unique words ‚Äî no relation to `een` and `twee`. From 13 onward you'll see a `-tien` ending (the Dutch `teen`): `dertien, veertien, vijftien...` Notice the small spelling shifts in `dertien` (not `drietien`) and `veertien` (not `viertien`) ‚Äî Dutch trimmed the awkward consonants.

callout.body: `Elf` (11) and `twaalf` (12) are unique ‚Äî there's no shortcut pattern. Memorize them as two-word flashcards and you're past the irregular hump.

sections.cutting_intro: Let's start at the start. Dutch one-through-ten is close enough to English that you'll feel cocky ‚Äî enjoy the easy stretch, because 21 is where it gets weird.

quick_check.question: Which two Dutch numbers from 1-20 are completely unique (no clear pattern with the others)?
quick_check.explanation: Correct ‚Äî `elf` (11) and `twaalf` (12) don't follow the `-tien` pattern that starts at 13. Memorize them on their own.

sections.heading: The big inversion: 21-99
sections.body:
Here it is ‚Äî the wall every English speaker hits. Dutch builds compound numbers BACKWARDS. The ones digit comes first, then `en` (and), then the tens. 21 isn't `twintig-een` (twenty-one); it's `een-en-twintig` ‚Äî literally `one-and-twenty`.

This isn't optional and it isn't a regional thing ‚Äî it's how all of Dutch (and German) works. 47 is `zeven-en-veertig` (seven-and-forty). 99 is `negen-en-negentig` (nine-and-ninety). Your brain wants to start with the bigger number, the way English does. Dutch demands the opposite. Drill until the flip is automatic.

The decades themselves you'll recognize: `twintig, dertig, veertig, vijftig, zestig, zeventig, tachtig, negentig`. Note `tachtig` (80) doesn't follow the obvious `acht-tig` ‚Äî it's irregular, like English `eighty` losing the `t` of `eight`.

table.caption: Sample compound numbers, with the inversion

callout.body: This catches EVERY English speaker. Your brain wants to say `vijfentwintig` as `twenty-five`, but you must train the flip ‚Äî small number first, then `en`, then big number. Phone numbers, prices, addresses ‚Äî they all use this. There's no avoiding it.

sections.cutting_intro: Here it comes. From 21 onwards, Dutch flips the order ‚Äî small number first, then the tens. Your instincts will fight you. Train through it.

quick_check.question: How do you say 25 in Dutch?
quick_check.explanation: Yes ‚Äî `vijfentwintig`. Small number (`vijf`) + `en` + tens (`twintig`). The English instinct to lead with `twenty` is wrong here.

sections.heading: 100, 1000, and beyond
sections.body:
Once you've conquered the 21-99 inversion, the larger numbers are a relief. `Honderd` (100), `tweehonderd` (200), `driehonderd` (300) ‚Äî just stack the multiplier in front. `Duizend` (1000) works the same: `tweeduizend, drieduizend`. A million is `miljoen`.

For numbers in between, you usually just say the parts in order: 250 = `tweehonderd-vijftig`, 1500 = `vijftienhonderd` (`fifteen-hundred` ‚Äî Dutch happily uses hundred-multiples up to about 1900, especially for years). Note: for any number under 100 inside a larger number, the inversion still applies. So 345 is `driehonderd-vijfenveertig` ‚Äî three-hundred + five-and-forty.

examples.breakdown: vijftien (15) + honderd ‚Äî Dutch often uses this form up to 1900
tweeduizend (2000) + zes-en-twintig (26) ‚Äî inversion still kicks in on the under-100 part

callout.body: Years in speech often use the hundred-form: 1985 is usually `negentienhonderd vijfentachtig` (nineteen-hundred + five-and-eighty), not `eenduizend...`. From 2000 onwards, the thousand-form takes over: `tweeduizend...`.

sections.cutting_intro: Big numbers are easier ‚Äî stack and go. Just watch out for the under-100 inversion sneaking back inside larger numbers.

quick_check.question: How would a Dutch speaker most naturally say the year 1985?
quick_check.explanation: Right ‚Äî `negentienhonderd vijfentachtig` (nineteen-hundred + five-and-eighty). For years between 1100 and 1999, the hundred-form is the default in everyday speech.

sections.heading: Telling time: half past goes BACKWARDS
sections.body:
Dutch time is the second wall, and it catches people just as hard as the number inversion. The on-the-hour, quarter-past, and quarter-to parts work like English ‚Äî but the HALF-hour is inverted. `Half drie` does NOT mean half past three. It means halfway TO three ‚Äî which is 2:30.

The logic: Dutch speakers think of the next hour as the target. So `half drie` = halfway to three = 30 minutes before 3:00 = 2:30. `Half tien` = halfway to ten = 9:30. Every English speaker gets this wrong the first ten times. Wire it into your brain now.

Quarter-past and quarter-to use `over` (past) and `voor` (to), and these work intuitively. `Kwart over twee` = quarter past two (2:15). `Kwart voor drie` = quarter to three (2:45). The 24-hour clock (`veertien uur` = 14:00) is common in writing ‚Äî trains, appointments, official documents.

callout.body: `Half drie` is 2:30, NOT 3:30. This trips up everyone, every time. When you hear `half X`, mentally subtract 30 minutes from X. `Half negen` = 8:30. `Half twaalf` = 11:30. Always pointing TO the next hour, never away from the previous one.

sections.cutting_intro: Brace yourself. Dutch half-hours run backwards from English. `Half drie` is 2:30 ‚Äî halfway TO three, not halfway PAST three. Drill this hard.

quick_check.question: A friend says `Ik kom om half acht`. When are they arriving?
quick_check.explanation: 7:30! `Half acht` = halfway to eight = 30 minutes before 8:00 = 7:30. The Dutch are always counting toward the next hour.

sections.heading: Days, dates, and common patterns
sections.body:
The days of the week are `maandag, dinsdag, woensdag, donderdag, vrijdag, zaterdag, zondag` ‚Äî Monday through Sunday. Dutch weeks start on Monday (like most of Europe), and the names are visibly related to English: `maandag` = moon-day, `zondag` = sun-day. Watch the pronunciation of `woensdag` ‚Äî it's roughly `WOONS-dach`.

Months line up with English: `januari, februari, maart, april, mei, juni, juli, augustus, september, oktober, november, december`. Note the lowercase ‚Äî Dutch capitalizes only proper nouns (names, place names, languages), and days/months don't qualify. Writing `Maandag` or `Januari` with caps is a beginner tell.

Dates are always written and spoken in `day-month-year` order: `15 maart 2026` (not `March 15`). When you read official forms ‚Äî appointments, leases, application deadlines ‚Äî they'll all follow this order. American month-first format will look wrong and feel wrong; Europe is uniform on this.

examples.breakdown: all lowercase in writing, unlike English
day first, then month ‚Äî opposite of American format
geboren = born; date is day-month-year throughout

examples.hindi: de winkel sluit om zes uur
examples.english: the shop closes at six o'clock
examples.breakdown: om + time = at that time; uur = hour/o'clock

callout.body: Dutch dates always go day-month-year ‚Äî `15-03-2026`, never `03-15-2026`. When filling in forms (gemeente paperwork, lease agreements, doctor's intake) this is what you'll see. Year-month-day (`2026-03-15`) also appears on official digital records, but never month-first.

sections.cutting_intro: Days, months, and dates ‚Äî the home stretch. The patterns are gentle but watch the order on dates: day always comes before month here.

quick_check.question: How would a Dutch form display 8 December 2025?
quick_check.explanation: Right ‚Äî `8/12/2025` (day-month-year). Dutch and most of Europe lead with the day. The American month-first format would look genuinely confusing on a Dutch form.

theory.wrap_up: Master 1-20 first, then drill the backwards 21-99 construction until it's automatic ‚Äî that's half the battle. The half-hour time-telling logic is the second wall, and the only way through it is deliberate practice (`half drie` = 2:30, repeat until it stops fighting you). Once those two patterns are wired in, the rest ‚Äî dates, days, prices, years ‚Äî is just stacking pieces. The phrases below give you realistic ordering, asking-time, and date-reading practice.
